title: Old Doctrines and New Paradigms
primary_topics: new paradigms requiring well-rounded judicial education
political law in the cyber age
sub_topics: Judge Learned Hand on the liberally educated jurist (verbatim quotation)
Tañada v. Angara (May 2, 1997) — Panganiban ponencia on WTO
Filipino First clause as non-self-executing
Manila Prince Hotel v. GSIS (February 3, 1997) — self-executing Art XII Sec 10
Mirpuri v. CA (1999) — Paris Convention as self-executing treaty
Tatad v. Secretary of Energy — RA 8180 Oil Deregulation Law voided 9-2
Big Three oligopoly (Petron, Shell, Caltex)
tariff differential / minimum inventory / predatory pricing as anti-competitive
Pimentel v. Aguirre — LGU fiscal autonomy / IRA
Meralco v. Province of Laguna — provincial taxing power
ABS-CBN v. Comelec — exit polls as protected free expression
Vasquez v. CA — truth as complete libel defense; actual malice doctrine for public officials
Death Penalty Law (RA 7659) constitutionality
People v. Echegaray — Panganiban dissent on death penalty
Echegaray v. Secretary of Justice — lethal injection
Five international anti-death-penalty treaties (Universal Declaration, ICESCR, ICCPR, two Optional Protocols)
Leo Echegaray February 5, 1999 execution as legally erroneous
Time magazine US DNA-exoneration data
Secretary of Justice v. Lantion (Mark Jimenez extradition)
Ople v. Torres (1998) — national ID card / right to privacy
Frivaldo v. Comelec — citizenship and elections
Mercado v. Comelec — dual citizenship vs dual allegiance
Bush v. Gore + Loong v. Comelec — automated elections
Santiago v. Comelec / PIRMA v. Comelec — RA 6735 initiative
Veterans Federation Party v. Comelec — four party-list parameters (Panganiban ponencia)
Niemeyer formula rejection (Washington apples / Guimaras mangoes metaphor)
Oposa v. Factoran — intergenerational right to environment
Tano v. Socrates — environmental ordinances
EDSA II oath-taking and Court Resolution AM 01-1-05-SC
Batiquin v. CA and Ramos v. CA — medical malpractice via res ipsa loquitur
Garcia Rueda v. Pascasio — four elements of medical negligence (duty, breach, injury, proximate causation)

stances.claim: In law and the magistracy, a combination of general education and specialized knowledge is essential — 'in no other profession is this more critical'; lawyers and magistrates must have well-rounded knowledge of history, literature, philosophy, humanities, social sciences, ethics, mathematics, IT, economics, medicine, biogenics, physics and natural sciences.

Constitutions 'should be interpreted to cover even future and unknown circumstances' — they must withstand 'the assaults of bigots and infidels but at the same time bend with the refreshing winds of change necessitated by unfolding events.' This is the doctrinal core of Tañada v. Angara.

While the Constitution has a bias in favor of Filipino goods, services, labor, and enterprises, it recognizes the need for business exchange on bases of equality and reciprocity — Filipino-First and economic-nationalism provisions are not self-executing and must be read together with other constitutional pronouncements for balanced development.

Combinations in restraint of trade and unfair competition are absolutely proscribed both against State and private sector — 'monopolistic or oligopolistic markets deserve our careful scrutiny and laws which barricade the entry points of new players should be viewed with suspicion.' (Tatad v. Secretary of Energy)

The Death Penalty Law (RA 7659) is unconstitutional — the 1987 Constitution 'abolished the death penalty from our statute books… did not merely suspend or prohibit its imposition' and granted 'a new right: the constitutional right against the death penalty, which is really a species of the right to life'; Congress failed to meet the constitutional requirements of 'heinousness' and 'compelling reasons.'
stances.rhetorical_move: dissent-recapitulation + structured-doctrinal critique (six points enumerated in fn 44)

stances.claim: RA 7659 collides with five international treaties (Universal Declaration, ICESCR, ICCPR, two Optional Protocols) — the International Bill of Human Rights — plus UN Commission on Human Rights Resolution 1997/12 calling on members to abolish the death penalty; the death penalty 'has no place in our statute books' since 'errors in its imposition become nightmarishly irreversible.'
stances.rhetorical_move: international-law layered argument + DNA-exoneration empirical evidence

stances.claim: The Echegaray execution (February 5, 1999) was legally erroneous — RA 7659's qualifying circumstance of 'common law spouse of the parent of the victim' was not alleged in the Information; the constitutional right 'to be informed of the nature and cause of the accusation' requires that special qualifying circumstances increasing the penalty to death must be both *alleged* AND *proven*.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal-procedural critique + 'in the Great Beyond, even a post facto correction will not resurrect him'

stances.claim: Truth is a complete defense in libel suits where the offended party is a public official or figure — conviction requires proof that defamatory words were (1) false AND (2) made with knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard (Vasquez v. CA, Mendoza). This 'avant garde' ruling reverses prior jurisprudence.
stances.rhetorical_move: actual-malice doctrinal endorsement + 'avant garde Court' framing

stances.claim: Exit polls are a species of protected free expression — Comelec's total prohibition during 1998 elections was grave abuse of discretion; 'narrowly tailored counter-measures may be prescribed by the Comelec so as to minimize or suppress incidental problems… without transgressing the fundamental rights of the people.' (ABS-CBN v. Comelec, Panganiban ponencia)
stances.rhetorical_move: ponencia-self-citation + free-expression evolution narrative (speech → press → strike/picket → broadcast → internet/teleconferencing)

stances.claim: Filipino citizenship in election cases should be read so that 'the popular will, as much as practicable, is upheld' — Frivaldo's repatriation was valid and citizenship is required only upon assumption of office, not at filing of certificate of candidacy or on election day.
stances.rhetorical_move: voter-will primacy framing in election-citizenship + 'reluctant hinges' (Batacan citation) but now relaxed

stances.claim: The Philippine party-list system 'is a unique paradigm which demands an equally unique formula' — the Niemeyer formula used in the German Bundestag does not transplant 'in toto here, because of essential variances'; 'just as one cannot grow Washington apples in the Philippines or Guimaras mangoes in the Arctic.'

In the e-age, there is 'an unmistakable shift of power from the state to the private sector' — globalization, deregulation, liberalization, privatization, plus the clamor for political/fiscal autonomy of LGUs, mean 'in the 21st century, the role of the state will be reduced.'

In two areas — the death penalty and due process for workers — the SC has yet to catch up with the cyber age; by ruling that due process applies only against government and not private power, the Court has turned a blind eye to the fact that 'corporate behemoths and private individuals may now be sources of abuses and threats to constitutional rights.'
stances.rhetorical_move: structured-self-critique + identification of two doctrinal lag-areas

register_markers: section headers (Liberal Education and Legal Expertise; The New Economy; Tañada v. Angara on Globalization; etc.)
verbatim case quotation framed with italics
Latin (ponencia, ponente, res ipsa loquitur, ipso facto, jus sanguinis, jus soli, sub judice, sui generis)
decision_framework_signals: applies liberal-education-plus-specialization as judicial-formation framework (Hand citation)
uses 'old doctrines meeting new paradigms' as the chapter's organizing analytic device
applies grave-abuse-of-discretion doctrine to constitutional review (Tañada v. Angara, Tatad)
frames Constitution as both stable (against bigots/infidels) and adaptive (refreshing winds of change)
uses non-self-executing-provisions doctrine to harmonize Filipino-First with WTO membership
applies anti-monopoly framework as economic-constitutional principle
uses 'must be alleged AND proven' doctrine for special qualifying circumstances in capital cases
applies actual-malice doctrine to libel cases involving public figures
frames death penalty as 'a species of the right to life' to be strictly construed against the State
applies international-treaty layered argument (Universal Declaration + ICESCR + ICCPR + Protocols) on the death penalty
uses res ipsa loquitur as evidentiary-burden-shifting in medical malpractice
frames party-list system as needing 'unique formula' rejecting wholesale-import of foreign formulas
applies voter-will primacy in election-citizenship cases (Frivaldo)
uses comparative-jurisdictions framing (US, EU, ASEAN) for global-paradigm analysis

Chapter 13 of this book (Estrada v. Desierto)
Henry J. Abraham, Justices and Presidents (1992 ed.)
notable_anecdotes: Justice Melencio Herrera inviting him to lecture at the Centenary Lecture Series — he addressed a 'jampacked audience in the Court's Session Hall on February 13, 2001'
Reading the entire 36-volume Uruguay Round of Multilateral Trade Negotiations plus books and treatises on globalization to write the Tañada v. Angara ponencia
Recalling 'former Court of Appeals Justice Delfin Fl. Batacan, my political law professor in law school more than forty years ago' for the 'reluctant hinges' citizenship metaphor
Davide and Panganiban inhibiting from Estrada v. Desierto (referenced as 'still sub judice' at time of lecture)
GMA's January 20, 2001 EDSA Shrine oath-taking by Davide at 12:29 pm, confirmed unanimously by the Court two days later
